policy_statement: By 2024, pass a residential time-of-sale policy requiring an electrification plan prioritizing water and space heating, indoor air quality, and electric safety, and record presence/absence of gas service for each property (BO.2-10).
mechanism_description: The policy requires a residential time-of-sale electrification plan (prioritizing water and space heating) and records gas service status, supporting a shift from gas equipment to electric systems and reducing on-site fossil fuel use in homes.
primary_causal_pathway: Building equipment choice & retrofit timing → ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion (gas) → ↓ direct CO₂e (plus ↓ upstream methane leakage) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Home sale transactions become a set intervention point where owners must create an electrification plan (often guiding future capital decisions) and the jurisdiction records whether a building has gas service, nudging/structuring upgrades away from gas water/space heating toward electric heat pumps or other electric appliances.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Electrification eliminates or reduces combustion of natural gas (or other fossil fuels) in residential equipment, cutting direct CO₂ emissions and methane-related climate impacts from fuel supply chains; any remaining emissions shift to the electricity system and typically decline as the grid decarbonizes.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in building-sector GHG emissions from space and water heating (and potentially cooking/drying if included), contributing to lower cumulative emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: Requiring/triggering electrification planning at time of sale accelerates replacement of gas end-uses with electric systems, reducing on-site fossil fuel combustion and associated GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The primary effect is reducing fossil fuel combustion in buildings by shifting end-use equipment from gas to electric, which directly lowers GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism can co-deliver indoor air quality, safety, and energy-cost benefits, those are co-benefits; the biggest climate-relevant effect is emissions reduction (Mitigation), not adaptation, resource efficiency, or nature-based outcomes.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electrification increases electricity demand on a high-carbon grid, near-term net reductions could be smaller, but the mechanism’s intent and dominant pathway still targets fossil fuel combustion reduction; planning/recordkeeping is an enabling policy but its climate significance derives from the induced fuel switch.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any “time-of-sale electrification planning/record gas service” mechanism is fundamentally aimed at accelerating fuel switching away from on-site fossil fuels, making mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a specific policy achieves reductions via mandates vs. disclosure vs. incentives, and the magnitude/timing of benefits, depends on policy design and local grid mix—these are policy-level details and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a jurisdiction’s implementation is purely informational (plan only, no follow-through) the realized emissions impact may be limited; nonetheless the dominant intended pathway remains mitigation via enabling electrification and reducing on-site fossil fuel use. If electrification is paired with resilience goals (e.g., outage-ready upgrades), adaptation could be a secondary co-benefit but not the primary mechanism.
key_indicators: “pass a residential time-of-sale policy”; “requires an electrification plan”; “prioritizing water and space heating”; “recording of the presence or absence of gas service for each property”
co_benefits: Indoor air quality improvements; improved electric safety; better access to emergency response information
instance_edge_case_notes: Contains an information/registry component (“recording… presence or absence of gas service”), but the dominant instrument is the mandatory time-of-sale planning requirement rather than standalone disclosure.
